coredata.dc:title: High-capacity adsorbent/sensor from nylon 6 derived carbon dots on SiO2 substrate via one-step surface grafting
coredata.dc:creator: Thongsai, Nichaphat
Supchocksoonthorn, Preeyanuch
Dwyer, Jonathan H.
Wei, Wei
Sun, Jian
Gopalan, Padma
Paoprasert, Peerasak
coredata.dc:description: The fabrication of nanoscale hybrid materials by modifying surfaces with photoluminescent nanoparticles is important for the development of technologically advanced functional surfaces and interfaces. In this work, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) were grafted onto SiO2 substrate using a simple one-step thermal annealing method. Two-step methods based on silane chemistry using amidation reaction between CDs and amine-terminated surfaces, and ring-opening reaction between CDs and glycidyl-terminated surfaces, were also developed. The thermal annealing method resulted in a complete monolayer of CDs on SiO2 substrate. The successful grafting by different methods also confirmed that the surface of CDs consisted of –COOH, –NH2, and –OH groups, which have been a subject of debate. The CD-grafted layer was then applied for metal ion sensing and adsorption, showing selective adsorption of Cu2+ with a high adsorption capacity of 2.4×10−5 mg·cm−2. Additionally, functionalization of the CD-grafted layer was also demonstrated toshow itstailoring ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nylon 6
Carbon dots
Grafting
SiO2 substrate
Thermal annealing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of APTES and GOPTMS substrates
Grafting of CDs onto SiO2 substrates
CD grafting on APTES and GOPTMS substrates
Metal ion adsorption on CD-grafted substrates
Metal ion adsorption in CD solution
Cu2+ adsorption capacity of CD-grafted surface
Functionalization of long alkyl chains on CD-grafted substrates
UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission properties
Metal ion adsorption and functionalization with long alkyl chain

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) XPS survey spectrum, and high resolution XPS of (c) C1s, (d) N1s, and (e) O1s signals of nylon-derived CDs.

(a) TEM images of nylon-derived CDs, inset: HR-TEM image of nylon-derived CDs, (b) AFM image of CDs, (c) height profile of selected line from AFM image, (d) UV–Vis spectrum, (e) fluorescence emission spectra, and (f) UV–Vis absorption and emission stability of nylon-derived CDs measured at the excitation of 372 nm.

AFM images and height profiles of (a) APTES-terminated surface, (b) CDs functionalized on APTES-terminated surface, (c) GOPTMS-terminated surface, (d) CDs functionalized on GOPTMS-terminated surface, (e) piranha-cleaned silicon substrate, and (f) CDs thermally annealed onto piranha-cleaned silicon substrate.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of CD-grafted SiO2 substrate using thermal annealing method, and its high resolution XPS of (b) N1s and (c) C1s signals.

AFM height images and height profiles of CD-grafted SiO2 substrates following thermal annealing for (a) 6 h, (b) 24 h, (c) 48 h, and (d) 96 h, and (e) Contact angles of CD-grafted samples at different annealing times.
AFM height images and height profiles of CD-grafted samples at annealing temperatures of (a) 110 °C, (b) 160 °C, and (c) 220 °C.

(a) Schematic of Cu2+ adsorption by CD-grafted substrate, (b) XPS survey scan of CDs after adsorption of metal ions, (c) high resolution XPS scan in Cu2p region, (d) fluorescence emission spectra, and (e) relative fluorescence intensity of CD solutions in the presence of different metal ions.
High resolution XPS spectra of CD-grafted substrate after Cu2+ adsorption for (a) 24 h and (b) 48 h.

(a) Reaction scheme of preparation of stearoyl-functionalized CD substrate, AFM height images, and height profiles of CD-grafted substrates (b) before and (c) after functionalization with stearoyl chloride, and (d) contact angles of stearoyl-CD-grafted SiO2 substrate after immersion in DI water for 7 days.
Schematic of CD grafting on SiO2 substrates.
Schematic of surface grafting of CDs via carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups.

head.ce:title: High-capacity adsorbent/sensor from nylon 6 derived carbon dots on SiO2 substrate via one-step surface grafting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani 12120, Thailand
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science and Technology, Thammasat University, Pathumthani 12120, Thailand

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, WI 53706, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, WI 53706, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, WI 53706, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Wisconsin-Madison, Madison, WI 53706, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fabrication of nanoscale hybrid materials by modifying surfaces with photoluminescent nanoparticles is important for the development of technologically advanced functional surfaces and interfaces. In this work, photoluminescent carbon dots (CDs) were grafted onto SiO2 substrate using a simple one-step thermal annealing method. Two-step methods based on silane chemistry using amidation reaction between CDs and amine-terminated surfaces, and ring-opening reaction between CDs and glycidyl-terminated surfaces, were also developed. The thermal annealing method resulted in a complete monolayer of CDs on SiO2 substrate. The successful grafting by different methods also confirmed that the surface of CDs consisted of –COOH, –NH2, and –OH groups, which have been a subject of debate. The CD-grafted layer was then applied for metal ion sensing and adsorption, showing selective adsorption of Cu2+ with a high adsorption capacity of 2.4×10−5 mg·cm−2. Additionally, functionalization of the CD-grafted layer was also demonstrated toshow itstailoring ability.

ce:section.ce:para: The fabrication of nanoscale hybrid materials combining organic and inorganic materials opens up ways for investigating surface and interface phenomena, such as adsorption and charge and energy transfer, and achieving novel functions in important applications in electronics, sensing, biomedicine, and catalysis [1]. Such nanoscale hybrid materials can be simply fabricated by preparing organic layers onto inorganic substrates. There are two main methods for creating these organic layers: physical adsorption and chemical grafting. Physical adsorption exploits weak-to-mild interactions, mainly the electrostatic force, hydrogen bonding, and hydrophobic interactions, to create a thin layer of adsorbed molecules [2]. In contrast, chemical grafting chemically attaches the molecules to the surface via the formation of covalent bonds [3]. Covalent bonds between organic materials and inorganic surfaces provide much more chemically and physically stable layers than the physisorbed ones [4,5]. Chemical grafting therefore provides greater chemical stability to the organic layers. However, it is often a more complex procedure, or requires appropriate chemical reactions between the organic and inorganic materials. The need for simple grafting methods that produces organic layers with high quality and stability is the driving force behind the development of efficient, well-controlled chemical grafting strategies.

Modification of inorganic substrates through the attachment of molecules is crucial for the introduction and control of functions that are not otherwise obtained from the inorganic surfaces. Ultrathin layers of photoluminescent materials have potential uses as in high-performance light-emitting diodes, sensors, or scaffolds for various applications [6,7]. Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional photoluminescent nanomaterials with a range of unique properties, are superior to inorganic quantum dots in a number of facets, offering tunable fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, non-blinking, and high aqueous solubility and have garnered wide attention in research community [8–12]. As they can be readily synthesized from a range of carbon precursors, they offer simple and low-cost approaches to large-scale production [13,14]. The grafting of CDs as active layers on inorganic substrates is thus an important step towards extending their applications and assessing molecular-scale interfacial phenomena.

Several studies have reported the fabrication of CD thin films and layers on substrates. The electrodeposition of a CD/chitosan composite onto gold electrodes was reported by Wang et al., who demonstrated the electrically-controlled release of the CDs [15]. Mahen et al. prepared CD/epoxy composite thin films by spin-coating onto glass substrate [16], while Guo et al. demonstrated self-assembling CD monolayers on oil–water interfaces through physisorption. Transparency and hydrophobicity could be controlled by varying the number of CD layer [17]. Kouloumpis et al. reported the preparation of graphene/CD hybrid thin films using a modified Langmuir–Schaefer method. Again, the transparency could be controlled by varying a number of layers [18]. These studies have demonstrated multiple methods for preparing CD thin layers with optically tuning properties and applications, but all were based on the use of composites and physical adsorption. Despite being rising star nanomaterials for a number of applications, there have been few attempts to graft CD layers onto inorganic substrates, and all of them involved multi-step process based on silane or amidation reactions. Xu et al. reported the functionalization of CDs on silica-shell encapsulated amino-passivated cadmium telluride and applied the result to Hg2+ sensing [19]. Cai et al. grafted silica with silanized CDs for use as a packaging material [20]. Zhang et al. reported the grafting of a CD layer onto indium tin oxide with applications in electrochemical imaging [21]. Zhao et al. reported the fabrication of CD-antifouling membranes for use in osmotic power generation. The CDs were anchored onto polydopamine films through an amidation reaction [22]. It is known that silane and amide linkers are prone to hydrolysis and thickness of the silane-grafted layer is also difficult to control due to the multilayer formation of silanes [23]. The development of one-step grafting method, or surface linkers with better chemical stability and controllability for CDs will therefore expand a range of applications.

In this work, facile strategies were developed for grafting CDs on SiO2 substrate. SiO2 substrate is the substrate of choice in this study as it is widely used in various applications. The current study is also the first report of synthesis of CDs from nylon. Nylon is one of the most widely used polymers, with annual global production amounting to several million tons [24]. If improperly disposed of, the waste products pose a range of environmental problems. The conversion of nylon to technologically useful nanomaterials is therefore desirable. We then developed three grafting strategies for the preparation of nylon-derived CD layers on SiO2 surface using two different linkers: silane and hydroxyl. Two types of silane linkers were employed: 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES) and (3-glycidyloxypropyl)-trimethoxysilane (GOPTMS) (Scheme 1 ). Silane compounds can form covalently-attached self-assembled monolayers on oxide surfaces [25–27]. The attachment is formed via hydrolysis reaction of alkoxy groups, followed by the covalent bond formation [28]. However, the silane linkages are prone to hydrolysis even in mildly acidic or basic conditions [29], and required multi-step to fabricate the monolayer. A grafting strategy for preparing stable layers of CDs was therefore developed using the hydroxyl linkers from the SiO2 substrate, based on thermal annealing method. This method has been used for the preparation of functional, stable layers of rhenium-bipyridine dye on TiO2 and of polymers on silicon substrates [30–32]. These were shown to be stable in aqueous solutions for at least four weeks [31]. The current work is the first to demonstrate the use of thermal annealing to graft CDs onto a SiO2 substrate. We took advantage of CDs having hydroxyl groups which can be condensed with silanol groups to form ether linkages. Thermal annealing is a one-step method, in contrast with the two-step methods of silane chemistry. The grafted CD surface layers were then tested for metal ion sensing, adsorption and functionalization with long alkyl chains. Our studies demonstrated that CDs can be grafted onto inorganic surfaces using a range of methods and can be post-modified to tailor the surface properties. The resulting hybrid materials have potential applications in sensing, electronics, coating, and other fields. The surface grafting methods developed in this work also provided a straightforward approach to fabrication of functional surfaces/interfaces and nanoscale hybrid materials that can be applied to a wide range of, but not limited to, carbon nanomaterials and oxide surfaces.

Nylon 6 or cast nylon 6 without additive was purchased from Veangudom Phattana Ltd. All chemicals were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich unless otherwise noted and were used without further purification. Sulfuric acid (96% H2SO4) and nitric acid (65% HNO3) were obtained from Carlo Erba. Cellulose dialysis membrane (MWCO 1 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Labs. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout. Silicon [100] wafers with the thickness of 525
±
25 µm, and resistivity of 1.0–60.0 Ω·cm were purchased from Addison Engineering, Inc.

Functional groups within the CDs were characterized using Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). FT-IR spectrum was measured using a Spectrum 2000, Perkin Elmer spectrometer. Samples were prepared by mixing with KBr and pressing into discs. Surface topography of samples was recorded using a Bruker MultiMode 8 atomic force microscope (AFM) in tapping mode. Survey and high-resolution XPS spectra were obtained from an XPS AXIS ULTRADLD spectrometer (Kratos analytical, Manchester, UK). The absorption and emission properties of the CDs were investigated at a concentration of 0.05 g·L−1. UV–Vis absorption spectrum was recorded with a UV-1700 PharmaSpec spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). Fluorescence emission spectra were measured using an FP-6200 fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were obtained from a Tecnai G2 F20 (FEI Corporation) microscope. The zeta potential of the CDs was measured using a Nano Particle Analyzer (HORIBA, Nano Partica SZ-100) at 25 °C with a scattering angle of 173°. Thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using a TA Instruments Q500 with a heating rate of 10 °C·min−1.

The morphology and height of CDs on the substrates were measured using an Atomic Force Microscope (AFM). Root-mean-square (RMS) surface roughness values were also determined from AFM images using the Nanoscope Analysis 1.5 program. Static water contact angle measurements were performed using a Dataphysics OCA 15 optical contact angle system. The contact angles were measured after dispensing 7 µL of DI water at a rate of 0.5 µL·sec−1.

Cast nylon 6 (7 g) was prepared in small pieces that were then pyrolyzed in an evaporating dish at 250 °C for 6 h with 2.5 M H2SO4 (50 mL). After the reaction completed, a black solid was obtained. This was cooled to room temperature and transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. A HNO3 solution (2.5 M, 50 mL) was added to the autoclave, and hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C was applied for 6 h. The residues were then filtered out using Whatman paper No.1. The solution of CDs was neutralized with 10 M NaOH, then dialyzed using a dialysis membrane against DI water for three days to remove ions. A solid product was obtained after freeze-drying (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of APTES and GOPTMS substrates
ce:section.ce:para: Silicon substrates (1 × 1 cm2) were cleaned using a 98% H2SO4:30% H2O2 solution at a ratio of 3:1 v/v at 85 °C for 45 min. A thin layer of SiO2 was also formed upon cleaning. They were then rinsed with plenty of DI water and dried with N2.

Monolayers of (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) self-assembled on cleaned SiO2 substrates by immersion in APTES solution (23.5 µL) with anhydrous toluene (100 mL) under an argon atmosphere. The mixture was heated to 70 °C for 1 h in a water bath. The APTES-functionalized substrates were cleaned by ultrasonication in toluene (3 × 5 min), rinsed with toluene, dried by N2, and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

Self-assembled monolayers of (3-glycidyloxypropyl)trimethoxysilane (GOPTMS) on SiO2 substrates were similarly prepared by immersion in GOPTMS solution (11 µL) with anhydrous toluene (10 mL). The mixture was kept at room temperature (22 °C) for 6 h. The GOPTMS-functionalized substrates were cleaned by ultrasonication in toluene (3 × 5 min), rinsed with toluene, dried by N2, and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Grafting of CDs onto SiO2 substrates

ce:section.ce:para: CD aqueous solution (40 µL, 10 g·L−1) was drop-cast onto the cleaned SiO2 substrate and dried in a vacuum oven for 1 h. The substrate was then thermally annealed in a vacuum chamber at 160 °C for 6 h, 24 h, 48 h, and 96 h. After annealing completed, the CD-deposited substrates were rinsed several times with DI water, then ultrasonicated in DI water (3 × 5 min) to remove unreacted CDs. The CD-grafted substrates were dried with N2 and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CD grafting on APTES and GOPTMS substrates
ce:section.ce:para: Grafting of CDs onto the APTES-functionalized substrate was performed using an EDC coupling reaction. CD powder (0.0097 g) and disodium phosphate (0.17 g) were dissolved in DI water (10 mL), followed by the addition of 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 25 mg). The APTES-functionalized substrates were then immersed in the solution under mild stirring at room temperature for 24 h. After the reaction completed, the substrates were rinsed several times with DI water, then ultrasonicated in DI water for (3 × 5 min) to remove unreacted CDs. The CD-APTES substrates were blown dried with N2 and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

To prepare the CD-GOPTMS substrates, the GOPTMS substrates were immersed in CD solution (0.0097 g dissolved in 10 mL of DI water) for 24 h at room temperature in the presence of 1 M H2SO4 (50 µL). Finally, the CD-GOPTMS substrates were rinsed several times with DI water, then ultrasonicated in DI water for (3 × 5 min) to remove unreacted CDs. The CD-grafted substrates were blown dried with N2 and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion adsorption on CD-grafted substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-grafted substrates were immersed in metal ion solutions (20 mL, 200 µM) containing Cu2+ (CuCl2), Co2+ (CoCl2), Ni2+ (NiCl2), Fe2+ (Fe(NH4)2 (SO4)2), Fe3+ (FeCl3), Sn2+ (SnCl2), Mg2+ (MgCl2), Zn2+ (ZnCl2), and Na+ (NaCl) for 24 and 48 h under mild stirring at room temperature. They were then rinsed with DI water, then ultrasonicated in DI water for (3 × 5 min) to remove undeposited metal ion. The substrates were blown dried with N2 gas and kept in a vacuum oven prior to use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion adsorption in CD solution
ce:section.ce:para: We next investigated the selectivity of the CDs to metal ions: Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Na+. The metal ion solutions (1.5 mL, 100 µM) were mixed with CD solution (3.5 mL, 0.02 g·L−1) in separate vials and transferred to a quartz cuvette. The fluorescence emission spectra were then recorded at an excitation wavelength of 372 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cu2+ adsorption capacity of CD-grafted surface
ce:section.ce:para:
The CD-grafted substrate (1×1 cm2) was immersed in Cu2+ solution (2.5 ppm, 5 mL) for 48 h and then the remaining solution was then subjected to measurement using ionic coupled plasma optical absorption spectrometry (ICP-OES, Perkin-Elmer Optima 8000). The amount of remaining Cu2+ ion was then determined from the predetermined calibration curve using standard Cu2+ solutions (0.1–5 ppm). A bare SiO2 substrate was used as a blank sample. Three duplicate samples were measured. The amount of Cu2+ adsorbed on CD-grafted substrate was calculated using equation (1):
(1)
C
u
2 +
a d s o r p t i o n
capacity
( m g · c
m
- 2
)
=
( C
I
-
C
R
-
C
B
) × v o l u m e
o f
s o l u t i o n
( L )
a r e a
o f
s a m p l e
( c
m
2
)
where CI , CR , and CB are the initial concentration of Cu2+ solution, the remaining concentration, and the concentration of adsorbed Cu2+ on a blank SiO2 sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of long alkyl chains on CD-grafted substrates
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-grafted substrates were immersed in anhydrous N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 5 mL) and pyridine (0.076 mL) solution. Stearoyl chloride (2.04 g) solution in DMF (15 mL) was then slowly added under an argon atmosphere. The mixture was mildly stirred at 50 °C for 48 h. After the reaction completed, the substrates were cleaned by rinsing several times with DMF, then ultrasonicated in DMF (3 × 5 min). The substrates were dried and kept in a vacuum oven for further characterization.

Nylon 6, an abundant, low cost polymer, was used as precursor for the synthesis of CDs. Pyrolysis and hydrothermal methods were shown to produce fluorescent CDs when used in combination. When used separately, no fluorescent CDs were produced. This may be due to incomplete carbonization or CD nanoparticle formation. A two-step method was therefore used. Under pyrolysis in the presence of sulfuric acid, the nylon 6 depolymerized and turned into carbon char. When hydrothermal treatment was then applied, the char turned into CDs. The acidic conditions endowed these CDs with hydrophilic functional groups, making them highly soluble in aqueous solution.

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) revealed a broad signal from 3600 to 3200 cm−1, attributed to stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds of CDs. Signals at 2900 and 1390 cm−1 corresponded to the C–H stretching and C–H bending vibrational modes, confirming the presence of sp3-hybridized carbons. The presence of oxygen-containing groups was confirmed by CO and C-O stretching vibrations at 1610 and 1220 cm−1. The strong signal at 1610 cm−1 was assigned to CC stretching of sp2 aromatic carbon, which overlapped with the CO stretching [33]. Survey and high-resolution XPS analysis were used to investigate the elemental components of the CD structure. The survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 2b) confirmed the presence of oxygen (28.9%), nitrogen (2.59%), and carbon (60.5%) at binding energies of 532.9, 400.3, and 284.6 eV, respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of the C1s signal (Fig. 2c) revealed different types of carbon bond: CC/C–C, C-O, and CO at binding energies of 285.0, 286.5, and 288.7 eV. The high-resolution spectrum of the N1s signal (Fig. 2d) revealed three different nitrogen conformations at different binding energies: pyridinic nitrogen at 399.2 eV, pyrrolic nitrogen at 400.2 eV, and graphitic nitrogen at 401.7 eV [34,35]. The high-resolution spectrum of the O1s signal (Fig. 2e) confirmed the presence of C=O, C-O, and O=C-O at 531.4, 532.2, and 533.6 eV. From the FT-IR and XPS results, it can be concluded that the CDs contained nitrogen- and oxygen-containing groups. The zeta potential of the CD aqueous solution was found to be −57.9 mV, suggesting the presence of strongly negatively charged functional groups on the surface.

The morphology and size of the CDs were characterized using TEM and AFM techniques. The CDs were close to spherical in the TEM images with diameters of 4–6 nm, which is consistent with previously-reported polymer-derived CDs [36]. The HR-TEM image (Fig. 3 a) showed a lattice spacing of 0.38 nm, slightly larger than that of graphite due to the enlarging effects by the surface functional groups [37,38]. The AFM image (Fig. 3b) of CD-spin coated on SiO2 substrate similarly demonstrated that the CDs were spherical nanoparticles. The height profile from the AFM image (Fig. 3c) suggested a vertical dimension of 4–5 nm. Both TEM and AFM showed the same morphology with sizes in the range 4–6 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission properties
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–Vis spectrum (Fig. 3d) showed that the CDs exhibited strong UV absorption from 200 to 400 nm. The shoulder signal at 267 nm was attributed to the
π -
π
∗
transition of aromatic sp2 hybridization [39], consistent with the CC stretching vibration detected from the FT-IR spectrum. The maximum fluorescence emission wavelength was at 455 nm with a maximum excitation wavelength of 372 nm. The inset figure showed the blue fluorescence emission of the CD aqueous solution under UV irradiation (365 nm). The quantum yield was calculated to be 2.53% using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a standard solution. The fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 3e) confirmed excitation-dependent emission as the excitation wavelength changed from 320 to 440 nm, corresponding to the different emissive states of different surface functional groups [40]. The photostability of the CDs in aqueous solution under long-wavelength UV irradiation (365 nm, 20 W) was next investigated. UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence emission remained nearly constant after an exposure of 6 h (Fig. 3f). This suggested that our nylon-derived CDs possessed excellent photostability.

Three grafting strategies are proposed in this work: via the use of APTES and GOPTMS, and thermal annealing method. First, the piranha-cleaned silicon substrate was functionalized with APTES, forming an amine-terminated surface through silane linkers. Following functionalization, the AFM image showed an increase in RMS roughness from 0.186 to 0.252 nm and a contact angle from 0° to 56.7° (Fig. 4 a), confirming previous findings [41]. The amine-terminated surface was then subjected to amidation reaction with the CDs. Sphere-like particles with a diameter of approximately 3–4 nm appeared randomly on the surface. From the TEM and AFM characterizations described above, this indicated the presence and successful functionalization of CDs on the amine-terminated surface. An increase of RMS roughness to 0.907 and contact angle of 63.5° (Fig. 4b) suggested that the CDs conferred roughness to the surface after grafting. Despite the incorporation of hydrophilic, nitrogen-doped CDs, the increase in surface roughness dominated, leading to a slight increase in contact angle.

Piranha-cleaned silicon substrates were also functionalized with GOPTMS, forming a glycidyl-terminated surface through silane linkages. Following functionalization, the AFM image showed an increase in RMS roughness from 0.186 to 0.242 nm and a contact angle of approximately 0° to 52.6° (Fig. 4c), consistent with the previous study [42]. The glycidyl-terminated surface was then subjected to ring-opening reaction with the CDs, as the amino groups in the CDs were able to open up the glycidyl ring [21]. After this reaction completed, sphere-like particles with a diameter of approximately 4–5 nm appeared randomly on the surface, indicating successful functionalization of CDs on the glycidyl-terminated surface. An increase in RMS roughness to 0.548 and contact angle of 60.1° (Fig. 4d) further confirmed the success of the ring-opening reaction, leading to grafting of CDs on the glycidyl-terminated surface.

Although both APTES and GOPTMS functionalization proved promising for introducing CDs onto the SiO2 surface, a uniform dense monolayer was not obtained. While EDC-activated amidation and glycidyl ring-opening are relatively efficient reactions, the steric effect of the CDs may prevent complete coverage. The bulkiness of the CDs may hinder the ability of incoming CDs to react with the surface [43]. In addition, the two-step methods required long reaction times and multiple cleaning steps. For the first time, a one-step thermal annealing method was therefore applied to graft the CDs directly onto substrates. First, the CD solution was drop-cast on the piranha-cleaned silicon substrate to ensure a large amount of CDs on the surface. Applying the previously-reported procedure [31], thermal annealing was carried out at 160 °C for 24 h under vacuum. The hydroxyl of the CDs and the silanol group of the SiO2 substrate condensed to form an ether linkage. The temperature was kept at 160 °C under vacuum to minimize decomposition of the CDs as thermal gravimetric analysis suggested that the CDs were relatively stable up to 280 °C (Fig. S1). An AFM image indicates an increase of RMS roughness to 0.747 nm and the appearance of nanoparticles with a height of approximately 4 nm (Fig. 4f). The contact angle was found to be 66.5°, slightly higher than those on APTES- and GOPTMS-terminated surface, suggesting a higher surface coverage of CDs on the surface by thermal annealing method. It is noteworthy that the number of CDs on the APTES-, GOPTMS-, and thermally annealed surfaces were 156, 307, and 888 nanoparticles per 1.5 × 1.5 µm2. At the same reaction time of 24 h, thermal annealing indeed produced the densest layer of CDs. To further confirm the successful surface grafting of CDs, samples were characterized XPS measurement. The XPS spectra (Fig. 5 a) showed the presence of nitrogen atoms. The high-resolution N1s peak was also investigated (Fig. 5b). It showed a broad peak at about 398–403 eV, which is attributed to graphitic, pyridinic, and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms similar to the pristine carbon dots before grafting, directly confirming the presence of CDs on the surface.

The effects of varying the thermal annealing time were next investigated. The drop-cast CD on substrates were thermally annealed at 160 °C for 6, 24, 48, and 96 h. It was found that the RMS roughness and the number of CDs on the surfaces increased with time. Additionally, large agglomerates started to build up at annealing times of 48 and 96 h, as the CDs started to form multilayers with heights of approximately 6–8 nm. Counting the bright particles on the surfaces, there were 488, 888, 652, and 762 CD particles per 1.5 × 1.5 µm2 at 6, 24, 48, and 96 h, respectively (Fig. 6 a-d). The lower numbers of particles and an increase in surface roughness at 48 and 96 h than at 24 h suggested the formation of multilayers, which may be attributed to self-condensation of the CDs. As the annealing time was increased from 6 to 96 h, the contact angle increased from 60° to 72° (Fig. 6e). This was due to an increase in surface coverage and roughness, which increased the hydrophobicity.

The optimal annealing temperature for the fabrication of CD-grafted substrate was also investigated (Fig. 7 ). Temperatures of 110, 160, and 220 °C were applied at a fixed annealing time of 24 h. Based on the RMS roughness value, the surface coverage of CDs was low at 110 °C, compared to that of 160 °C annealing temperature. This temperature might be insufficient to condense the hydroxyl groups, forming an ether linkage. Similarly, few CDs were grafted onto the substrate at 220 °C, as prolonged annealing at high temperature may induce degradation of CDs. Contact angle results were also consistent with the RMS roughness. The CD-grafted SiO2 substrates prepared at 110 and 220 °C showed the lower contact angle compared to that of 160 °C. These results suggested that the annealing temperature of 160 °C is indeed optimal for grafting CDs on SiO2 substrates.

Types of functional groups present on CDs have long been a subject of debate. Characterization techniques and experiments suggest that CDs are carbon-based nanoparticles with oxygen- and nitrogen-containing functional groups at the surface [44,45]. The successful grafting of CDs onto APTES- and GOPTMS-terminated surfaces shown in this work indicated the presence of carboxyl (–COOH) and amino (–NH2) groups, as these groups are required for the amidation and ring-opening reactions, respectively. Similarly, the success of grafting by thermal annealing via the ether bond formation indicated that the CDs contained hydroxyl (–OH) groups. The CDs synthesized from nylon 6 in this work therefore carried carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on their surfaces (Scheme 2 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metal ion adsorption and functionalization with long alkyl chain
ce:section.ce:para: The CD-terminated surface was next tested for metal ion adsorption. CDs are known to act as metal ion probes and widely used for metal ion sensor in solution phase [34,46,47] due to the presence of oxygen- and nitrogen-containing groups which can chelate with the metal ions [48]. However, the use of CDs in solution phase as sensing probe cannot recover the metal ions. The CD-terminated surface can therefore function as sensors and, more importantly, as adsorbents for simultaneous recovery. First, to test the selectivity towards metal ions, the CD-terminated surface was immersed in a mixed solution of metal ions comprising Cu2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Sn2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Na+ for 24 h (Fig. 8 a). The metal salts were carefully chosen to avoid interference reactions between their metal ions and counter ions. After immersion and thorough cleaning, the substrate was characterized using XPS (Fig. 8b and c). No XPS signals of all metal ions except from Cu2+ were observed. Although the XPS signal of Cu2+ was weak, the results indicated that the CDs had the strongest affinity with Cu2+, possibly through the complexation between Cu2+ and carboxyl and amino binding sites on the CDs [49]. The weak XPS signal of Cu2p at 933 eV could be due to competitive binding with some of the metal ions. Metal ion adsorption by the CDs in solution was investigated using fluorescence spectroscopy to confirm these results (Fig. 8d and e). It was found that Cu2+ produced the most fluorescence quenching, indicating strong binding between the CDs and Cu2+, consistent with the surface adsorption results. This study showed that the CDs were competent solid-state probe for Cu2+ ions. They showed good selectivity towards Cu2+ in the presence of interfering metal ions.

The effects of immersion time were investigated by immersing the CD-grafted substrate in a pure Cu2+ solution for 24 and 48 h. From the XPS spectra, strong Cu2p signals were observed [50] (Fig. 9 ). Furthermore, as the immersion time was increased from 24 to 48 h, the intensity of the Cu2p signals almost doubled. Strong XPS signals indeed confirmed that the weak signals from previous experiment were due to the competitive binding among metal ions. This also suggests that Cu2+ adsorption onto CDs is diffusion-controlled, allowing adsorption to be controlled by immersion time. Using ICP-OES, the amount of Cu2+ adsorbed on the CD-grafted substrate was determined to be 2.4 ± 0.5×10−5 mg·cm−2, which is equivalent to approximately 2 ions·nm−2 and the highest among known adsorbents for Cu2+ [34]. Given a CD diameter of ~5 nm, the surface area of spherical CD (4πr2 where r is the radius) is about 78 nm2. Assuming that the bottom half of CD spherical particle is too steric to adsorb Cu2+, this means that one grafted CD particle can adsorb approximately 78 copper (II) ions. The high adsorption capacity of CD can be attributed to the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl and amino groups, which are excellent ligands for Cu2+ [34]. The CD-grafted substrate thus has the potentials to be used as a two-in-one Cu2+ sensor and adsorbent for simultaneous sensing and recovery. Copper is one of the most widely used metals in industry. In its most abundant ion form of Cu2+, it is involved in a number of metabolic and enzymatic processes, including the production of cytochrome C oxidase, tyrosinase, and superoxide dismutase [51]. However, exposure to high levels of Cu2+ can cause damage to the kidney, liver, and nervous systems [52,53]. The selective detection and removal of Cu2+ are therefore important. The CD-grafted substrate also has the potential as the solid-state ligand for recyclable catalysis.

To further explore the potential uses of the CD-grafted substrates, they were functionalized with stearoyl chloride, a long-chain hydrocarbon (Fig. 10 a). Following the functionalization with stearoyl chloride, morphology of the CD-grafted layer exhibited large agglomerates, which were not observed from the CD-grafted one (Fig. 10b and c). The RMS roughness and contact angle increased from 0.747 to 1.26 and 66.5° to 82.2°, respectively, suggesting successful functionalization of long hydrocarbon chains on the CDs. The stability of stearoyl-functionalized CD layer on SiO2 substrate was further investigated (Fig. 10d). Following immersion in DI water for 7 days, the contact angles remained relatively stable, indicating that the grafted layer was not hydrolytically destroyed. This confirms the potential of our CD-grafted surface as robust scaffolds for functionalizing molecules to be used in applications in electronics, sensing, catalysis, and biomedicine.

In this work, we demonstrated successful grafting of CDs onto SiO2 substrates by three different routes. The preparation of CD-grafted SiO2 substrates by these routes also confirmed the presence of –COOH, –NH2, and –OH groups on the CD surface. Thermal annealing to graft the CDs directly onto the SiO2 substrates resulted in a complete monolayer coverage. The CD-grafted surface showed selective detection and high adsorption of Cu2+, suggesting practical applications as two-in-one sensors and adsorbents. Post modification with functionalization with molecules was also demonstrated, confirming that the CD-grafted surface can be tailored to achieve novel properties and functions. The appreciable stability of the grafted layer suggested its uses as robust scaffolds for various applications in coating, electronics, biomedicine, recyclable catalysis, sensing, and other fields. Furthermore, the grafting strategies developed here may be used to tailor other oxide surfaces such as conductors and semiconductors or to include other electronic properties.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the funding from Thammasat University Research Unit in Carbon Materials and Green Chemistry Innovations and use of facilities and instrumentation supported by NSF through the University of Wisconsin Materials Research Science and Engineering Center (DMR-1720415). The authors also acknowledge the Central Scientific Instrument Center (CSIC), Thammasat University Center of Scientific Equipment for Advanced Research (TUCSEAR), Department of Chemistry, Department of Biotechnology, Faculty of Science and Technology, and Thammasat University. The authors also gratefully acknowledge the supports provided by Science Achievement Scholarship of Thailand, Commission of Higher Education, Ministry of Higher Education, Science, Research and Innovation, Thailand and The Royal Golden Jubilee Research Fund. Financial support from the Thailand Research Fund through the Royal Golden Jubilee Ph.D. Program (Grant No. PHD/0130/2559) is provided to P. Supchocksoonthorn and P. Paoprasert. Jonathan Dwyer acknowledges funding from NSF-CMMI award no. 1462771.
The raw data required to reproduce these findings will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mseb.2020.114692.
